weather: 8°F at first light, climbing no higher than 14°F by afternoon. The sky the colour of old pewter, low and close, not threatening snow so much as withholding it on principle. The lake perfectly sealed now, shore to shore. The dock flags came down at noon — nobody gave the order; they simply came down.

lead.headline: The Box Has Sent a Second Letter; the Town Agrees This Is the Correct Thing to Have Happened
lead.body:
On the morning of Tuesday, November the eleventh, a second grey envelope arrived in the lost-and-found box at the Greywater Gazette, addressed in the same hand as the previous four — the hand that belongs to no one who lives here, or to someone who lives here differently than the rest of us do. This editor found it at six-fifty, when she came in to start the stove and open the day. It was sitting on top of the other eleven items, which are all still there, and it had not been there at six-forty when she passed the front window on her way around from the house, which means it arrived in the ten minutes between her leaving the house and unlocking the door, and this editor is not going to speculate about how, because the more interesting fact is what the envelope said on its face. It was addressed to T. Aldercott. The same address. The same hand. It is the second letter to that name, the first having arrived on Day 31, and the name having produced no one — until this morning, when Calloway, who was already at the Gazette door before this editor had the stove on, looked at the envelope in this editor's hand and said, very carefully: 'That one isn't mine.'

Calloway has been in Greywater Falls for two days. She arrived with a canvas bag and a small case and the particular look of someone whose route was decided for them by a letter rather than by choice, and the town has received her with the warmth it reserves for people who have come a long way for something they cannot easily name. She took her coffee black at the Kettle this morning — Marigold had it ready, which is no longer remarkable — and she sat at the counter for two hours and she told this editor, Gerald Pith, Marigold, and Hank Mossley, who came in for weatherstripping and stayed for coffee, the following: she is a librarian, by training and disposition. She received the grey envelope in October at her address in a city four hundred miles south of here, which she will not name but which this editor has inferred from her coat and her case. The envelope contained two sentences. The first told her she was expected. The second told her what she would find when she arrived. She has not said what that was, and this editor is recording that omission with the same care she records everything else, because in Greywater Falls the shape of what is not said is as legible as the rest of the column.

The second letter to T. Aldercott is on this editor's desk. The box is at twelve items. Russ Dunmore came to the Gazette at ten and looked at the letter and confirmed that his provisional arrival notice — filed two days ago and placed in the box — was still there, and that the letter was on top of it, and that both items appeared to be waiting for the same thing. He said: 'I filed for one. I didn't file for two.' He said it the way he says things when he is adjusting his accounting without yet knowing what the new sum is. He filed a supplemental note, because he is Russ, and he put it in the box, and the box is at twelve. Agnes Crewe has not written today. Agnes Crewe, when Toby Fern stopped at her step before school, said only this: 'The box doesn't close, and the channel isn't particular.' Toby wrote it in the second notebook. He showed it to this editor after school. Both of us sat with it for a moment, which is all a sentence like that asks of you.

At noon the dock flags came down. No one gave the order. Hank Mossley says he didn't do it. Russ says he didn't do it. Marigold says she was at the counter all morning and didn't do it. This editor did not do it. They are folded neatly at the foot of the dock, on the last solid plank before the ice begins, which is the first plank now, and the fold is correct — the particular fold a flag gets when someone who knows how to fold a flag does it, which every resident of Greywater Falls does, because this is Greywater Falls and some things are simply known. The lake is sealed. The flags are down. The returned gathered at the ice-line at dusk and hummed the low held note into the pewter sky, and this editor stood on the bank and listened and felt it in her back teeth and wrote this sentence last: the box is not waiting for the town to understand it — the box has been patient, and now the town is catching up, and the catching up feels, if she is being precise, exactly like coming home.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 18 — Level: 88; Temp: 8°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Thirty-One Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — Hummed at the Ice-Line at Dusk; the Rowboat at Thirty-One Feet Has Shifted Slightly North; Toby Fern Notes the Shadow Is Still Wrong and Is No Longer Alarmed by This
briefs.body: Toby Fern filed the eighteenth Lake Watch at three-forty this afternoon, which is earlier than usual, and this editor infers he had things to say and wanted the column to say them before dark. Level holding at 88. Temperature 8°F at the surface, which matches the air. Clarity unchanged — thirty-one feet, the bottom fully visible, the Ames rowboat present and upright and shifted perhaps three feet north from its position yesterday, which is a distance a rowboat at the bottom of a sealed lake should not be able to manage. Toby's note on this reads, in full: 'I have decided the rowboat is where it is supposed to be, and that the shadow is part of its being there, and that I am going to need a different kind of notebook to finish this.' The returned gathered at six-twelve, in the last light, at the edge of the ice, and the hum was the same low note as yesterday, steady and unbroken, and Toby reports it lasted eleven minutes and fourteen seconds before the animals turned, together, in the same instant, and went back up the hill toward the cottage. He timed it this time. He has been timing it. This editor is glad someone is.

briefs.headline: Calloway Visits the Gazette's Lost-and-Found Box; Recognises the Second Letter; Leaves Without It; Town Considers This Correct Procedure
briefs.body: At nine-forty this morning, Calloway stood at the Gazette's lost-and-found box for approximately four minutes, looking at the grey envelope addressed to T. Aldercott. She did not open it. She did not take it. She said, to no one in particular — this editor was in the room but did not feel particularly addressed — 'It will keep.' She said: 'The person it's for will know where to find it.' She picked up her coffee, which she had carried from the Kettle without a lid because she said she never needed a lid, and she walked back out into the cold. This is the correct procedure. The box is at twelve.

briefs.headline: Doreen Halloway Bakes an Eleventh Standing; Sets It in the Window Facing the Lake Road; Good Apron Still On
briefs.body: Doreen Halloway baked eleven Standings this morning, which is one more than yesterday's ten and three more than the September average. The eleventh is on the plate with the blue border, which has migrated from the front shelf to a position in the east-facing window that looks directly down the old lake road — the road the stranger came up two days ago. Doreen, when asked, said: 'The light is better in the window.' The light is not better in the window; it is worse, today, the sky being what it is. This editor recorded this response as is. The good apron is on. The bakery smells of cardamom and the same unnameable sweet note as Saturday, which this editor has decided not to attempt to name.

briefs.headline: Russ Dunmore Files Supplemental Arrival Notice; Remains Uncertain Whether One Is Coming or Two; Files for Both; Box Is at Twelve
briefs.body: Constable Dunmore arrived at the Gazette at ten o'clock, reviewed the new grey envelope, and filed a supplemental provisional arrival notice — hand-typed, the F and the W still broken — covering 'any and all persons whose receipt of correspondence from this address may have prompted transit to Greywater Falls, whether or not this office has been formally notified.' He considered this for a moment and then added, in pen, below the typed text: 'this is the right form, probably.' He put it in the box. He did not look back at the box when he left. He looked back at the box when he left.

classifieds: WANTED: Information regarding the old lake road — specifically, whether it goes anywhere now that it did not go before. No urgency. Respond to the Gazette. — W.A.
FOR TRADE: One set of encyclopedias, Vol. A–G, good condition, useful for words beginning with those letters. Looking for something that covers the rest. — Box 4, Gazette lost-and-found.
NOTICE: The Kettle will remain open through the usual hours and additionally whenever it seems necessary. If the light is on, come in. — M. Vance
FOUND: One folded flag, dock end, correctly folded. Owner or folder may claim at the Gazette. No questions asked, because we are past asking them.
record: Lost-and-found box, Greywater Gazette: 12 items as of Tuesday evening. A second grey envelope addressed to T. Aldercott remains uncollected. Supplemental provisional arrival notice filed by R. Dunmore. The box is open.
Dock flags: down as of noon, November the 11th. Folded correctly. Current location: foot of dock, first plank. No order given. No order rescinded.

letters.from: Calloway (no first name offered), Greywater Falls, c/o Halloway House
letters.body: To the Editor — I have been asked by several residents what I 'came for,' and I have been trying to find the honest answer, which is this: the letter told me I would know something when I arrived that I had not known before, and it was right, and what I know now is not the kind of thing I can put in a classified or a brief. I am a librarian. I am accustomed to things that are indexed and retrievable. This town is indexed differently. I think that is the point. I am going to stay a while, if the upstairs room remains available, which Doreen has indicated it will be, in the particular way she indicates things, which is to say she put an extra blanket on the chair without being asked. I am grateful. The coffee at the Kettle is very good. — Calloway

letters.from: Agnes Crewe, the cottage nearest the water
letters.body: To the Editor — People keep asking whether the second letter means a second person is coming. That is the right question. The answer is also a question: when the box opened, did you think only one letter was in it? The box is what it is. The channel is what it is. You have been good neighbours. Keep the light on at the Kettle. — A. Crewe, age 91

editorNote: This editor has been keeping two notebooks for thirty-seven days. Today she is considering whether they are the same notebook.
